note: Data-center & generation PPAs. Deals are point-in-time (as of mid-2026) and will age — treat them as worked examples of structures, not a live deal tracker. Each carries a primary or reputable-trade source. 'level': 1=newcomer, 2=practitioner, 3=advanced.

demand.stat: US data-center electricity demand more than doubled (2.3x) from 2018 to 2024 and could reach 325–580 TWh by 2028 — up to roughly 12% of US electricity.
demand.source: LBNL — 2024 US Data Center Energy Usage Report

why: Data centers need large, firm, 24/7 power faster than the grid can build it. 'Speed-to-power' is now the binding constraint — pushing deals toward firm sources (nuclear, geothermal, gas), on-site generation, and direct ownership.

structures.name: Existing-plant nuclear restart / uprate
structures.what: Contract the output of a recommissioned or up-rated existing nuclear unit under a long-term (often ~20-year) PPA.
structures.whyClever: Delivers large, firm, carbon-free baseload that already has an interconnection — far faster than building new generation. Long tenor underwrites the restart capex.
structures.tradeoffs: Limited to the few restartable sites; needs NRC approval; multi-year lead time; can draw scrutiny over keeping the plant off the public grid.
structures.example: Microsoft × Constellation — Three Mile Island Unit 1 (Crane Clean Energy Center)

structures.name: Behind-the-meter co-location
structures.what: Site the data center directly next to a generator and take power on the generator's side of the grid meter, bypassing the transmission network.
structures.whyClever: Fastest path to large firm power — no new transmission, no full interconnection queue. The load and supply are physically married.
structures.tradeoffs: Raises FERC and cost-allocation questions about whether co-located load avoids paying for grid services it still relies on for backup. FERC rejected Talen's amended interconnection agreement for the AWS co-location in Nov 2024.

structures.name: Front-of-meter retail / grid-connected IPP
structures.what: Restructure a co-location deal as a grid-connected, front-of-meter arrangement where the generator becomes the licensed retail supplier to the load.
structures.whyClever: Keeps the commercial relationship and the firm nuclear supply while sidestepping the FERC co-location dispute — the load is a normal grid-connected retail customer.
structures.tradeoffs: The load pays grid charges it avoided under co-location; depends on a retail-choice (deregulated) market.
structures.example: Amazon × Talen — restructured June 2025 (17-yr, up to 1,920 MW)

structures.name: Small modular reactors (SMRs)
structures.what: Sign an offtake for the output of yet-to-be-built small modular reactors, often as a fleet across multiple units.
structures.whyClever: Firm 24/7 carbon-free power that can scale in increments; the corporate offtake gives the developer the demand certainty to build first-of-a-kind units.
structures.tradeoffs: First units are ~2030+; first-of-a-kind cost and schedule risk; needs NRC licensing.
structures.example: Google × Kairos Power (up to 500 MW, first SMR ~2030); Meta × Oklo / TerraPower

structures.name: Enhanced geothermal
structures.what: Offtake firm power from next-generation (enhanced) geothermal projects that engineer heat reservoirs in non-traditional locations.
structures.whyClever: Dispatchable, 24/7 carbon-free power that complements variable solar/wind — fills the firmness gap without nuclear lead times.
structures.tradeoffs: Still scaling; geographically constrained; newer technology risk.
structures.example: Google × Fervo (115 MW, Nevada); Meta × Sage Geosystems / XGS Energy (~300 MW)

structures.name: Bring-your-own-power (on-site generation)
structures.what: Build dedicated on-site generation — typically gas turbines, sometimes fuel cells — to energize the campus directly, ahead of or instead of a grid connection.
structures.whyClever: The fastest route to firm megawatts when the interconnection queue is multi-year. Common among neoclouds racing to deploy GPUs.
structures.tradeoffs: Carbon and air-permit exposure; not carbon-free unless paired with capture or clean fuel; capital and O&M sit with the developer.
structures.example: Crusoe (Stargate / Abilene) — ~29 GE Vernova gas turbines, ~1 GW

structures.name: 24/7 hourly carbon-free matching
structures.what: Procure clean energy so that carbon-free supply matches consumption every hour in every region, rather than netting it out across a year (annual/volumetric matching).
structures.whyClever: A far more rigorous clean-energy claim than annual RECs — it forces procurement of firm and storage resources, not just cheap midday solar.
structures.tradeoffs: Harder and more expensive; some analyses find hourly matching is not always the most cost-effective way to cut system-wide emissions versus targeting dirtier grids.
structures.source: DOE — Clean Energy Resources to Meet Data Center Demand

structures.name: Direct ownership / vertical integration
structures.what: Acquire or co-develop the generation (and sometimes the grid assets) rather than only contracting for its output.
structures.whyClever: Control over the build timeline — the buyer can co-locate generation and data center and move at its own speed instead of waiting on a counterparty.
structures.tradeoffs: Capital-intensive; pulls a tech company into the power business; concentration and execution risk.
structures.example: Google × Intersect Power ($4.75B, Dec 2024) — co-developing generation + data centers

structures.name: Frontier offtake (fusion)
structures.what: Sign an early offtake for a not-yet-commercial technology to help finance its first plant.
structures.whyClever: Signals demand and de-risks first-of-a-kind capital; positions the buyer for firm clean power if the technology matures.
structures.tradeoffs: High technology risk; long and uncertain timelines; mostly a strategic bet, not near-term supply.
structures.example: Microsoft × Helion — world's first fusion PPA (~50 MW, target 2028)

deals.note: Restarts Three Mile Island Unit 1 as the Crane Clean Energy Center; targeted online ~2027–2028; ~$1.6B restart capex.

deals.structure: Restructured from behind-the-meter co-location to front-of-meter retail after FERC rejected the co-location interconnection agreement
deals.note: ~$18B; Talen acts as AWS's licensed retail electricity provider in PA, sourcing from the grid.

deals.structure: First corporate SMR fleet offtake; first reactor ~2030, more through 2035
deals.note: World's first corporate agreement to buy power from multiple SMRs.

deals.structure: Geothermal PPA (Nevada) plus a $4.75B move into Intersect Power to co-develop generation and data centers
deals.note: Pairs firm geothermal with vertical integration for speed-to-power.

deals.structure: Multi-partner nuclear procurement following a Dec 2024 RFP for 1–4 GW of new nuclear
deals.note: One of the largest corporate nuclear procurement efforts in US history.

deals.structure: Firm geothermal PPAs (Texas, New Mexico) for baseload data-center power
deals.note: Complements Meta's 12+ GW of renewables with dispatchable clean power.

deals.structure: World's first fusion power purchase agreement; target online 2028
deals.note: A frontier bet to finance first-of-a-kind fusion.

deals.structure: Bring-your-own-power on-site generation for the Stargate / Abilene (Lancium) campus
deals.note: Speed-to-power play; campus scaling to ~1.2 GW. Stargate also adds on-site solar + storage.

deals.structure: Acquire the power-secured data-center footprint outright rather than contract for it
deals.note: Vertical integration to lock in gross power capacity.
